retrieved_docs.title: Serotonin Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The 5-HT1 receptors are inhibitory G-protein-coupled receptors and have 5-subtypes: 5-HT1A, 5-HT1B, 5-HT1D, 5-HT1E, 5-HT1F. Several medications are prescribed to target these receptor subtypes. Buspirone targets 5-HT1A, and the antimigraine agents sumatriptan and zolmitriptan target 5-HT1B and 5-HT1D. [9] The 5-HT2 receptor family are excitatory G-protein-coupled receptors with 3 subtypes: 5-HT2A, 5-HT2B, and 5-HT2C. The 5-HT2A receptor is a common target of most second-generation antipsychotic medications that antagonize the receptor. Notably, 5-HT2A antagonism is suspected to shunt serotonin to available 5-HT1A receptors not blocked by antipsychotics, which may be a risk factor for precipitating serotonin syndrome. [12] 5-HT2C is targeted by the weight-loss drug lorcaserin (withdrawn from the United States market in 2020). [13] The 5-HT3 receptor is an excitatory ligand-gated ion channel most relevant for initiating emesis when activated. Ondansetron is a 5-HT3 receptor antagonist that directs antiemetic properties. The 5-HT4 receptor is a stimulatory G-coupled-protein receptor that increases gastric motility. Prucalopride is a 5-HT4 agonist that treats constipation. The 5-HT5 receptors are G-protein-coupled receptors comprised of 2 subtypes, 5-HT5A, and 5-HT5B, but have little known function and are unknown targets of medications. The 5-HT6 and 5-HT7 receptors are also stimulatory G-protein-coupled receptors, with some antidepressants and antipsychotics modulating activity through off-targeting, but no medications currently target 5-HT6 or 5-HT7. The etiology of serotonin syndrome results from cellular toxicity of excess target post-synaptic 5-HT stimulation. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Gastroparesis -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Other off-label medications aimed at symptom relief include domperidone, centrally-acting antidepressants, and the serotonin type 4 (5-HT4) partial agonist tegaserod. [32] Domperidone is a dopamine-D2 antagonist that works similarly to metoclopramide to improve gastric emptying and decrease nausea and vomiting. The FDA restricts domperidone use in the United States; it is currently under review as an investigational drug. The initial dosage of domperidone is usually 10mg 3 times daily but can be increased to 20 mg per dose. Significant adverse effects of domperidone include cardiac arrhythmias and hyperprolactinemia; drug reactions are prevalent. Monitoring the QTc interval in patients taking domperidone is recommended; avoid or discontinue use if the QTc exceeds 450 ms.

retrieved_docs.title: Constipation  -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: An additional option, prucalopride, a selective agonist of serotonin 5-HT 4 receptors, induces and increases the amplitude of colonic high-amplitude propagated contractions, thereby increasing bowel movement frequency and relieving constipation symptoms. The recommended dose is 2 mg once daily in adults and 1 mg daily in individuals with significant renal impairment. Side effects include headache, abdominal pain, nausea, and diarrhea. Prucalopride is contraindicated in patients with Crohn's disease, ulcerative colitis, intestinal perforation or obstruction, and toxic megacolon. [29] Patients with disorders such as colonic inertia may require total abdominal colectomy with ileorectal anastomosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Antiemetics, Selective 5-HT3 Antagonists -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the mechanism of action of 5-HT3 receptor antagonists. Identify the approved indications for 5-HT3 receptor blockers. Determine the potential adverse events associated with 5-HT3 receptor antagonists. Develop strategies for the interprofessional healthcare team to improve care coordination and communication to improve patient outcomes when using 5-HT3 receptor antagonists. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Antiemetics, Selective 5-HT3 Antagonists -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Serotonin binding to 5-HT3 receptors triggers nausea and vomiting. [3] Serotonin 5-hydroxytryptamine-3 (5-HT3) receptor antagonists exert their activity on receptors located peripherally on gastrointestinal (GI) vagal nerve terminals and centrally in the chemoreceptor trigger zone (CTZ). [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Laxatives -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Prokinetic agents: Cisapride and tegaserod work as agonists of 5-hydroxytryptamine receptors. [5] They work on intrinsic neurons, releasing acetylcholine and inducing mucosal secretion. [7] However, cisapride has been withdrawn from the market due to concerns about severe cardiovascular adverse effects. Tegaserod is available under new investigational drug processes. Prucalopride is a selective, high-affinity 5-HT 4 agonist. [8] ATI-7505 and velusetrag are agents under investigation as well. [7] Lubricants: Mineral oil aids the passage of stools by its lubricating action throughout the intestines. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Antiemetics, Selective 5-HT3 Antagonists -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The 5-HT3 receptor antagonists available on the US market include dolasetron, granisetron, palonosetron, and ondansetron. [1] Dolanestron, granisetron, and ondansetron are first-generation 5-HT3 receptor antagonists. Palonosetron is a second-generation 5-HT3 receptor antagonist. [2] Tropisetron and ramosetron are not available in the US. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Alosetron -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: An umbrella systematic review evaluating pharmacologic treatments for irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) assessed patient outcomes associated with 5-HT-3 antagonists, 5-HT4 agonists, guanylate cyclase-C agonists, antispasmodics, and alosetron. According to the review's findings, alosetron exhibited beneficial effects, including relief of global IBS symptoms. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Antiemetics, Selective 5-HT3 Antagonists -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Elimination: Ondansetron undergoes extensive hepatic metabolism to inactive metabolites. [14] The elimination half-life for ondansetron is about 4 hours. Granisetron undergoes 12% renal excretion. [15] The elimination half-life for granisetron is about 4 to 6 hours. Dolasetron has a half-life of less than 10 minutes, while hydrodolasetron has a half-life of about 7 hours. [3] Unchanged dolasetron undergoes less than 1% excretion in the urine. [16] Polanosetron is longer acting than the first-generation 5-HT3 receptor antagonists and has an elimination half-life of about 40 hours. [19] [3] Polanosetron undergoes about 80% renal excretion, with 40% of the drug being excreted unchanged.

retrieved_docs.title: Ondansetron -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Ondansetron is a selective 5-HT3 serotonin-receptor antagonist used for its antiemetic properties. It is one of the four FDA-approved 5-HT3 serotonin-receptor antagonists used to combat nausea and vomiting, including granisetron, dolasetron, and the second-generation drug, palonosetron. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Gastroparesis -- Treatment / Management -- Investigational Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple prospective treatments for gastroparesis are under investigation. Granisetron, a 5-HT3 antagonist administered as a transdermal patch, appears to improve gastroparesis-related nausea and vomiting. [36] Similarly, a randomized control trial of aprepitant, a neurokinin-1 receptor antagonist, showed symptom improvement in gastroparesis patients. [37] Cannabinoids, such as dronabinol, have been considered in the treatment of gastroparesis symptoms but have not been studied in clinical trials. However, cannabinoids can also delay gastric emptying. Acupuncture has proven effective for diabetic gastroparesis in randomized control trials. [31] Other investigational therapies undergoing evaluation include elamorelin, a pentapeptide ghrelin-receptor agonist; the 5HT-4 receptor agonists prucalopride and velusetrag; and metoclopramide nasal spray. [31] [38] [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Metoclopramide -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Metoclopramide works by antagonizing central and peripheral dopamine-two receptors (D2) in the medullary chemoreceptor trigger zone in the area postrema, usually stimulated by levodopa or apomorphine. It achieves this by decreasing the sensitivity of visceral afferent nerves that transmit from the gastrointestinal system to the vomiting center in the area postrema in the chemoreceptor trigger zone. [13] In addition to antagonizing dopamine receptors, metoclopramide is an antagonist at 5HT3 (type 3 serotonin receptors) and an agonist at 5HT4 receptors. [14] [15] Metoclopramide also blocks the antiperistaltic effects of apomorphine, allowing metoclopramide to slow apomorphine's inhibition of gastric emptying, thereby accelerating gastric emptying by increasing the amplitude and duration of esophageal contractions. Consequently, it increases the resting tone of the lower esophageal sphincter while simultaneously relaxing the duodenal bulb and pyloric sphincter, thereby increasing the peristalsis of the duodenum and jejunum. [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Antiemetics, Selective 5-HT3 Antagonists -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: The American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) guidelines suggest administering granisetron extended-release subcutaneous injection or granisetron transdermal patch to patients undergoing a multiday chemotherapy cycle instead of administering a 5-HT3 receptor antagonist each day of the multiday cycle. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Antiemetics, Selective 5-HT3 Antagonists -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Antiemetics, Selective 5-HT3 Antagonists -- Adverse Effects -- One Percent to 10%
retrieved_docs.content: 5-HT3 receptor antagonists carry a risk of dose-dependent QT prolongation. [35] The Food and Drug Administration (FDA) issued a warning about the risk of QT prolongation associated with a single dose of 32 mg intravenous (IV) ondansetron. [35] Most published literature shows that the risk of QT prolongation with 5-HT3 antagonists is clinically insignificant in healthy patients. A review of several studies conducted on healthy patients concludes that dolasetron carries the highest risk of QT prolongation, followed by ondansetron and then granisetron. [36] Further studies are needed to determine the cardiovascular safety of 5-HT3 antagonists in patients with cancer, polypharmacy, and other cardiotoxic medications. [36] Experts recommend using the antiemetic agent with the least known QT prolongation, such as polanosetron, in patients at risk of QT prolongation. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Corticobasal Degeneration -- Treatment / Management -- Symptomatic Management
retrieved_docs.content: Constipation and urinary disturbances may become prominent as a part of possible autonomic dysfunction in CBD. Constipation may respond to increased physical activity, adequate hydration, fiber supplementation, and pharmacologic therapy with stool softeners or osmotic laxatives. Stimulant laxatives should only be used short-term. Medications like guanylate cyclase c agonists (linaclotide) and selective 5-hydroxytryptamine receptor 4 agonists (prucalopride) may need to be prescribed in refractory cases of constipation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 853
retrieved_docs.content: Alosetron and cilansetron are pharmacologic agents classified as serotonin receptor antagonists for functional gastrointestinal disorders. Both drugs belong to this therapeutic class, which targets serotonin receptors to modulate gastrointestinal motility and sensitivity, thereby providing symptom relief in conditions such as irritable bowel syndrome. Recognizing alosetron and cilansetron as members of the serotonin receptor antagonist group helps clinicians select appropriate receptor‑targeted therapies for patients with functional gastrointestinal complaints.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2949
retrieved_docs.content: Serotonin receptor antagonists for functional gastrointestinal disorders constitute a class of drugs indicated for the treatment of functional gastrointestinal disorders, and alosetron is identified as a specific member of this drug class.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1390
retrieved_docs.content: Drugs for functional gastrointestinal disorders constitute a therapeutic category that encompasses both serotonin receptor antagonists for functional gastrointestinal disorders and antispasmodics in combination with psycholeptics. Serotonin receptor antagonists for functional gastrointestinal disorders represent a subclass of these drugs and include cilansetron as a specific member. This organization of drug classes clarifies how various pharmacologic agents, such as cilansetron and combined antispasmodic‑psycholeptic formulations, are grouped under the broader umbrella of treatments for functional gastrointestinal conditions, aiding clinicians in selecting appropriate therapies for patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1946
retrieved_docs.content: Tropisetron is classified as a serotonin (5‑HT₃) antagonist, belonging to the drug class that blocks the 5‑HT₃ receptor. Serotonin (5‑HT₃) antagonists, including tropisetron, are used clinically to inhibit serotonin‑mediated signaling that can trigger nausea, vomiting, and related gastrointestinal symptoms, thereby improving patient comfort and outcomes in settings such as chemotherapy, postoperative care, and radiation therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 1014
retrieved_docs.content: Metoclopramide is classified as a member of the propulsives group, indicating its role as a gastrointestinal motility‑enhancing agent. In addition, metoclopramide belongs to the dopamine antagonist class, reflecting its pharmacologic action of blocking dopamine receptors. Promazine is also identified as a dopamine antagonist, sharing this mechanism of action with metoclopramide. Together, these relationships highlight that both metoclopramide and promazine act as dopamine antagonists, while metoclopramide uniquely combines this activity with its membership in the propulsives category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2333
retrieved_docs.content: Alosetron is classified as a serotonin receptor antagonist that is employed in the treatment of functional gastrointestinal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1643
retrieved_docs.content: Papaverine and its derivatives, identified as drugs for functional gastrointestinal disorders, and serotonin receptor antagonists for functional gastrointestinal disorders are both classified as therapeutic agents used to manage functional gastrointestinal disorders. These two drug categories represent distinct pharmacologic approaches within the broader group of medications indicated for functional gastrointestinal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1311
retrieved_docs.content: Serotonin (5HT3) antagonists constitute a class of antiemetics and antinauseants that act by blocking 5‑HT3 receptors to prevent and treat nausea and vomiting, particularly in settings such as chemotherapy, radiation therapy, and postoperative recovery. Tropisetron is a specific drug that belongs to this class, exemplifying the therapeutic application of serotonin (5HT3) antagonism. By inhibiting the serotonergic pathways that trigger emesis, both the broader class of serotonin (5HT3) antagonists and individual agents like tropisetron play a crucial role in improving patient comfort and outcomes during treatments associated with high emetogenic risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 336
retrieved_docs.content: Drugs for functional gastrointestinal disorders comprise several therapeutic subgroups that are employed to manage symptoms of functional GI conditions. One important subgroup is serotonin receptor antagonists for functional gastrointestinal disorders, which includes agents such as alosetron that act by blocking serotonin receptors to reduce visceral hypersensitivity and improve bowel function. Another distinct subgroup consists of antispasmodics in combination with psycholeptics, a formulation that pairs muscle‑relaxing antispasmodic agents with central nervous system‑acting psycholeptic drugs to provide both peripheral and central relief of gastrointestinal spasm and discomfort. Together, these categories illustrate the diverse pharmacologic strategies—serotonin receptor blockade and combined antispasmodic‑psycholeptic therapy—used within the broader class of drugs for functional gastrointestinal disorders to achieve symptom control and enhance patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2332
retrieved_docs.content: Serotonin (5HT3) antagonists are classified as antiemetics and antinauseants, meaning they are pharmacologic agents used to prevent or treat nausea and vomiting by blocking the 5‑hydroxytryptamine type 3 (5HT3) receptors involved in the emetic pathway.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1925
retrieved_docs.content: Carboxylic acid and/or carboxylic acid derivative‑containing product is a chemical class that includes both phenyl salicylate and tropisetron. Phenyl salicylate, a salicylate ester, and tropisetron, a 5‑HT₃ receptor antagonist used as an antiemetic, each possess a carboxylic acid functional group or a derivative of it, linking them to this broader product category. Recognizing that these two distinct compounds share the carboxylic acid moiety helps explain similarities in their physicochemical properties, formulation considerations, and potential metabolic pathways, which is important for pharmaceutical development and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 167
retrieved_docs.content: as a parent compound for tropisetron, demonstrating a shared chemical backbone despite their distinct therapeutic indications. Together, these relationships show how prostaglandins integrate biochemical pathways, drug classification systems, and multiple therapeutic areas, while serotonin‑3 antagonists exemplify the diversification of carboxylic‑acid‑based structures into both analgesic and antiemetic agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 715
retrieved_docs.content: Serotonin (5HT3) antagonists constitute a class of antiemetics and antinauseants. Tropisetron is a member of this class, so it functions as an antiemetic and antinauseant through 5‑HT3 receptor blockade. In addition to its pharmacologic classification, tropisetron belongs to the broader chemical group “carboxylic acid and/or carboxylic acid derivative‑containing product,” a category that also includes phenyl salicylate. Both tropisetron and phenyl salicylate share the structural feature of containing a carboxylic acid or its derivative, linking them chemically, while tropisetron’s role as a serotonin (5HT3) antagonist defines its therapeutic use. Recognizing these overlapping chemical and therapeutic relationships helps clinicians understand the dual classification of agents like tropisetron and the broader family of carboxylic acid derivatives that encompass both therapeutic drugs and other compounds such as phenyl salicylate.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2233
retrieved_docs.content: Ondansetron is a medication that belongs to the class of serotonin (5‑HT3) receptor antagonists, which block serotonin receptors to prevent nausea and vomiting. Antipsychotics constitute a separate pharmacologic category used to treat psychotic disorders, and acepromazine is an example of a drug that is classified as an antipsychotic (a phenothiazine tranquilizer). Although both ondansetron and acepromazine are drugs, they are members of distinct therapeutic classes with different mechanisms of action; ondansetron is not an antipsychotic, while acepromazine exemplifies the antipsychotic class. Recognizing these differences aids clinicians in selecting appropriate treatments and avoiding confusion between medication categories.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 668
retrieved_docs.content: Drugs for functional gastrointestinal disorders constitute a therapeutic group that is classified as propulsives, agents that enhance gastrointestinal motility. Within this group are several distinct subclasses: papaverine and its derivatives, serotonin‑receptor antagonists, synthetic anticholinergics (quaternary ammonium compounds), and antispasmodics used in combination with psycholeptics. Each of these subclasses is also regarded as a propulsive drug class, reinforcing their shared purpose of promoting intestinal transit. The propulsive class includes the well‑known pro‑kinetic agent metoclopramide. Specific members of the serotonin‑receptor antagonist subclass are alosetron and cilansetron, both of which act by blocking serotonin receptors to alleviate functional gastrointestinal symptoms. The synthetic anticholinergic subclass comprises agents such as hexocyclium, and it also encompasses methanthelin as a member of this chemical group. Overall, the hierarchy links the broad category “drugs for functional gastrointestinal disorders” to its constituent subclasses and individual agents, all unified by their role as propulsives that facilitate gastrointestinal motility in patients with functional GI conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2785
retrieved_docs.content: Alosetron and bismuth subcitrate are medications that may be used to treat الإِسْهال (diarrhea). Alosetron, a selective 5‑HT3 receptor antagonist, is indicated for severe diarrhea‑predominant irritable bowel syndrome, while bismuth subcitrate, a bismuth‑based compound, possesses antidiarrheal and gastroprotective properties. Both agents represent pharmacologic options for managing الإِسْهال, underscoring the importance of targeted drug therapy in the treatment of diarrheal conditions.
